summary: Fournier reflects on the tension between technical correctness and practical outcomes in engineering decisions. She recounts fighting her boss over an API design choice she knew was right, only to later find herself as a manager making the same pragmatic tradeoffs with her own developers. The post explores how being technically correct often matters less than shipping, building consensus, and driving business results. She acknowledges the frustration engineers feel when overruled, while explaining why experienced leaders choose pragmatism over rightness.
key_points: Being technically right doesn't automatically mean the fight to prove it is worth having
The time spent debating correctness often exceeds the value of the correct solution
Managers must weigh technical decisions against non-technical factors like adoption, consensus, and business impact
Engineers often don't believe their managers truly understand their technical position, even when they do
Experience tends to shift perspective from fighting for rightness to optimizing for results
money_quotes: I told him we shouldn't negotiate with terrorists.
But he also used it, which was all that mattered to my boss, and in retrospect, was more important for the success of the project than my sense of rightness.
No matter how much I say, you're right, but it doesn't matter, it doesn't sink in.
They tell me that they understand that technical decisions are sometimes a series of non-technical tradeoffs, but in this case, why can't I see that this is just the right way to do it?
You will be worn down from fighting for right, and you will be driven to produce the best results with the most consensus and not distract everyone with the overhead of debating every decision.
I still resent my boss for not fighting for me, but I understand where she was coming from.
key_insight: Technical correctness is often less important than adoption, consensus, and business outcomes—a lesson engineers only truly internalize when they become managers themselves.
